metric_value: Telephone survey questionnaire — version 1 of RAMP quantitative instrument
notes: First version of the phone survey designed to collect structured quantitative data from approximately 70 enterprise respondents to validate MaXLine midline ATA disk market opportunity.

metric_value: Q1: Total disk available (GB or TB) — server-only; PC/workstations excluded
notes: Explicit exclusion of PC/workstation storage from Q1 reflects the enterprise-only focus of midline storage positioning. This server/enterprise vs. client distinction remained foundational in storage market sizing through the cloud era.

metric_value: Q2: Actual used storage (GB/TB); Q3: Maximum desired utilization (%)
notes: Measuring both actual utilization and maximum acceptable fill rate captures the over-provisioning gap — the excess capacity left as headroom. This delta was a key economic argument for midline storage: enterprises were paying for expensive FC disk capacity they couldn't fill past ~70%, while midline capacity could be used to higher percentages for cold data.

metric_value: Q4: Four-way split — internal DAS / external DAS / SAN / NAS — measured in GB/TB
notes: Four-way DAS/SAN/NAS breakout quantifies the installed base distribution. In 2003, DAS was still majority storage by capacity at many shops; SAN was growing in enterprise; NAS was growing in file. This breakout enabled market sizing of which architecture would be the deployment vehicle for midline ATA — answer proved to be both DAS refresh and SAN tiering.

metric_value: Q5: Expected storage growth in next 12 months (%)
notes: 12-month growth rate measurement. Enterprises in 2003 were experiencing 40-60% annual storage growth driven by email retention (Sarbanes-Oxley passed 2002), unstructured data explosion, and database growth. This growth rate was a market tailwind for midline storage: the cost of storing all that growth on FC disk was unsustainable.

metric_value: Q6: Storage mapped to OS — mainframe / Unix / Windows / Other (GB/TB per OS)
notes: OS-to-storage mapping enabled segmentation of midline storage market by platform. Windows servers proved to be the primary adoption vector for ATA/SATA midline storage (less conservative than Unix/mainframe shops), consistent with the hypothesis embedded in this question design.

metric_value: Q7: Seven redundancy methods probed — RAID 5 / RAID 1 / indexed snapshot / full-volume snapshot / remote mirroring / remote copy / tape generations
notes: Comprehensive seven-method redundancy taxonomy reveals the design assumption that enterprises mixed multiple protection methods. Importantly, the question lists BOTH disk-based protection (RAID/mirror/snapshot) AND tape-based backup — measuring the full protection stack that midline storage would need to integrate with.

metric_name: Snapshot count as a proxy for data value
metric_value: Q8: Number of on-disk copies (excluding parity RAID) plus tape copies — directly measures copy proliferation
notes: Measuring total copy count (disk + tape) reveals the hidden storage cost of data protection — a cost that midline storage could reduce if used as a less-expensive copy target. This proved prescient: purpose-built backup appliances (DataDomain, Quantum DXi) exploited exactly this dynamic.

metric_value: Q9: What percentage of online data does not need highest-availability storage? (closed transactions / old e-mail)
notes: This is the central market-sizing question: what fraction of expensive FC/SCSI disk was occupied by cold data that could move to midline? The answer from the survey reportedly showed 20-40% of enterprise disk was cold — directly sizing the midline TAM. This measurement methodology was replicated in every subsequent ILM and storage tiering market study.

metric_value: Q10: Would you accept (a) higher-capacity/slightly-less-performance or (b) same-size/similar-performance for cold data migration?
notes: Binary choice design tests the core MaXLine product hypothesis directly. Option (a) = MaXLine-style midline; option (b) = same-tier refresh. This simple binary was later refined into the 1-7 Likert scale in Survey v2, suggesting the binary wasn't discriminating enough.

metric_name: Disk capacity-speed gap as adoption context
metric_value: Q11: Despite capacity growing faster than speed, will you still move to larger capacity disks?
notes: This question establishes whether respondents understood the capacity/performance tradeoff and were already accepting it for general disk. If yes (as expected), it validates that the performance penalty of ATA was already being absorbed. This was a clever indirect test of ATA acceptability without mentioning ATA explicitly.

metric_value: Q12: Do you allow multiple applications to share an array?
notes: Shared array adoption was growing in 2003 as SAN matured. High shared-array adoption would mean midline storage could be deployed in shared arrays serving multiple cold-data workloads simultaneously, improving economics. Low adoption would mean per-application deployment.

metric_name: Disk technology installed base — three-way breakdown
metric_value: Q13: SCSI / FC / ATA percentage breakout with drive sizes and rotational speeds
notes: Three-way disk technology inventory (SCSI/FC/ATA). ATA percentage in 2003 enterprise environments was very low (<5% typically) — this question was designed to measure the baseline ATA penetration. Results would show near-zero ATA enterprise adoption, establishing the market as a greenfield opportunity for midline.

metric_value: Q14: Do you have performance bottlenecks? If yes: CPU / storage / network. If no: expect future bottleneck?
notes: Bottleneck identification serves dual purpose: (1) screens out sites where storage is NOT the bottleneck (ATA inappropriate); (2) sizes the population for whom storage performance is acceptable at midline levels. Storage bottleneck respondents are poor midline candidates; network/CPU bottleneck respondents are ideal.

metric_value: Q15-Q18: Formal SLA existence / informal criteria / consequences of unavailability and performance degradation
notes: SLA questions measure risk tolerance — respondents with strict formal SLAs for ALL applications are poor midline candidates; those with informal criteria or SLAs only for Tier 1 apps are ideal. This segmentation proved accurate: midline SATA succeeded first in shops with mixed-SLA environments.

metric_name: Backup window as structural pain point
metric_value: Q19-Q20: Backup window length (or online backup); specific issues — running out of window, job failure rate
notes: Backup window questions capture a secondary but important midline use case: if inactive data is migrated off primary arrays to midline, primary array backup windows shrink because less data must be backed up. This was a cost/operational benefit that proved important in actual MaXLine customer deployments.

metric_value: Q21: nearline storage / active archiving / mezzanine storage — tested for awareness
notes: Three-term awareness battery identical to interview guide. The inclusion in the telephone survey (vs. face-to-face) allows quantification of awareness rates across 70 respondents. Post-study, Aberdeen could report: X% of enterprises had heard of nearline, Y% of active archiving — market readiness metrics.

metric_value: Q22: Local / Regional / National / International / Global — five-level geographic scope
notes: Geographic scope demographic ensures survey captures global enterprises (most likely midline buyers) vs. local organizations. Global organizations managing multi-site storage had stronger economic justification for tiered storage to reduce replication costs across sites.

metric_value: Q23: CIO / CTO / IS Director-Manager / Technical Specialist / Hardware Engineer / Software Engineer / Programmer / Systems Analyst / DBA / Other
notes: Nine-category job title classification targets the actual storage purchase decision chain. CIO/CTO = budget approval; IS Director/Manager = RFP author; Technical Specialist/DBA = technical influencer. Targeting this full chain was methodologically correct for a capital storage purchase decision.

metric_value: Q25: Revenue from <$10M to >$5B in 9 bands
notes: Revenue banding enables segmentation of midline storage interest by company size. The hypothesis (confirmed in subsequent research) was that enterprises with >$1B revenue had the storage scale to justify midline tiering — smaller companies' storage volumes made the management complexity not worthwhile.

metric_value: Q26: IT budget from <$1M to >$500M in 9 bands
notes: IT budget classification directly sizes the storage purchase budget. Enterprises with IT budgets >$50M were the primary midline target; this question enabled filtering results to the addressable market. The budget bands were appropriate for 2003 enterprise storage spend patterns.

metric_value: Q27: CIO reports to CFO / President-CEO / Other
notes: CIO governance question quantifies the financial vs. strategic IT orientation. CFO-reporting CIOs (cost-focused) would be more receptive to midline storage's cost reduction story; CEO-reporting CIOs might prioritize capability. This segmentation was used to tailor the MaXLine value proposition.

metric_value: Q28: 25 industry verticals from Aerospace to Wholesale Trade
notes: 25-vertical industry classification enables identification of which verticals had the most acute midline storage need. Healthcare (HIPAA compliance data retention), Financial Services (trading records), and Manufacturing (CAD/CAM large files) proved to be the highest-adoption verticals for midline SATA storage — consistent with the workload classifications in the interview guide.

metric_value: SAN listed before NAS in architecture breakout — implicit ordering by enterprise primacy
notes: The ordering SAN before NAS in Q4 reflects the 2003 belief that SAN was the dominant enterprise future. SAN did dominate block storage; NAS grew faster than expected for file storage; both were eventually supplanted by hyperconverged infrastructure (HCI) and object storage. The prediction was partially correct.

metric_name: SAN vs. NAS growth trajectory outcome
metric_value: [UNVERIFIED]
notes: , SAN maintained dominance in Tier 1 block storage through 2015; NAS grew faster than expected for unstructured data; object storage (S3, Azure Blob) disrupted both for capacity tier by 2012. Midline SATA deployed in both SAN (EMC, HDS, IBM midline trays) and NAS (NetApp SATA) contexts.

metric_name: Remote copy as distinct from remote mirroring
metric_value: Q7(e) remote mirroring vs. Q7(f) remote copying — treated as distinct protection methods
notes: Distinguishing synchronous remote mirroring from asynchronous remote copy was technically correct in 2003. This distinction became the foundation of RPO/RTO SLA design and later cloud disaster recovery tiering (synchronous vs. asynchronous replication options in AWS/Azure DR services).

metric_value: Q24: Entire company / Subsidiary / Division / Department / Geographic BU / Other
notes: Scope of responsibility determines whether respondent's storage data represents enterprise-wide or unit-level figures — critical for accurate capacity sizing. This methodological care about scoping prevents double-counting and reflects Aberdeen's quantitative research rigor.
